title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
summary: Finding eigenvalues via the characteristic equation, eigenvectors, diagonalization, and applications.
content_html:
Definition

For square matrix A, scalar λ (eigenvalue) and non-zero vector v (eigenvector) satisfy:

Av = λv

Finding Eigenvalues — Characteristic Equation

det(A - λI) = 0

The resulting polynomial in λ is the characteristic polynomial. Its roots are the eigenvalues.

Finding Eigenvectors

For each eigenvalue λ, solve the homogeneous system:

(A - λI)v = 0

The non-zero solutions are the eigenvectors corresponding to λ.

Worked Example — 2×2

A = [3  1]
    [0  2]

Characteristic equation: det(A-λI) = (3-λ)(2-λ) - 0 = 0
→ (3-λ)(2-λ) = 0  →  λ₁ = 3, λ₂ = 2

For λ₁=3: (A-3I)v = [[0,1],[0,-1]]v = 0 → v₁ = (1,0)
For λ₂=2: (A-2I)v = [[1,1],[0,0]]v = 0 → v₂ = (-1,1)

Properties of Eigenvalues

  • Sum of eigenvalues = trace(A)
  • Product of eigenvalues = det(A)
  • A is singular ⟺ 0 is an eigenvalue
  • Eigenvalues of Aⁿ are λⁿ
  • Eigenvalues of A⁻¹ are 1/λ

Diagonalization

If A has n linearly independent eigenvectors (columns of P), then:

A = PDP⁻¹   where D = diag(λ₁, λ₂, ..., λₙ)

This is useful for computing powers: Aⁿ = PDⁿP⁻¹

Symmetric Matrices

Symmetric matrices always have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors from different eigenvalues.
